title: Treacle tart with lattice
subtitle: A classic British tart with golden syrup and breadcrumbs, lattice-topped
excerpt: Treacle tart is a British classic with a name that deceives: the filling is golden syrup, not treacle. Breadcrumbs are stirred in to absorb the syrup and give the filling body; a little cream and lemon juice cut the sweetness. A lattice pastry top is traditional.
sourceNotes: Treacle tart using golden syrup is documented in British household cookbooks from the late nineteenth century following the introduction of Lyle's Golden Syrup in 1881. The recipe appears in public-domain British cookbooks from the Edwardian era onward.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The tart is good warm or at room temperature. Keeps covered at room temperature for 2 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 170 degrees C; the lattice should be golden, the filling set but not bubbling

glossaryTerms.definition: Despite the name, treacle tart filling is made with golden syrup rather than treacle. Golden syrup is a refined sugar syrup with a pale amber colour and a mild, buttery sweetness; black treacle is dark, intensely bitter, and quite different in flavour. The name predates the distinction between the two products; when treacle tart became established, what was commonly called treacle was often lighter in colour than what we now call black treacle. The breadcrumbs in the filling absorb the syrup and give it body, preventing the filling from being a liquid pool.

content.text: is made with golden syrup. The lemon juice and cream prevent the filling from being purely sweet; the breadcrumbs absorb the syrup and set it to a thick, fudgy consistency. The pastry here is a sweet shortcrust; the lattice requires patience but is straightforward once the pastry is rolled and chilled.

Combine the flour and icing sugar. Rub in the cold butter until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Mix in the egg yolk and cold water until the dough just comes together. Divide off one-third for the lattice. Wrap both pieces and chill for 30 minutes. Preheat the oven to 190 degrees C (conventional). Roll the larger piece to line a 23 cm tart tin with a removable base. Press into the corners and trim the overhang, leaving 1 cm proud of the rim. Chill for 15 minutes, then line with baking paper and baking beans and blind-bake for 15 minutes. Remove the beans and paper and bake for 5 minutes more until pale golden.

Warm the golden syrup gently in a saucepan until it loosens. Remove from the heat and stir in the breadcrumbs, cream, lemon juice, and lemon zest. Leave to stand for 5 minutes; the breadcrumbs will swell. Pour into the warm pastry case. Roll the reserved pastry piece into a rectangle and cut into 1 cm strips. Lay strips in a lattice pattern over the filling, pressing the ends to the pastry rim. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the lattice is golden and the filling is just set and barely wobbling. Leave to cool in the tin on a rack for at least 30 minutes before removing. Serve warm with cream.
